description: E-commerce site went down after a sudden traffic spike caused by a marketing campaign. The outage was caused by a configuration error in cluster resource management which prevented more service instances from starting even though hardware resources were available.
raw_input:
10:02 UTC - PagerDuty: CRITICAL - High error rate on web-gateway. 502/503 errors spiking on checkout page.
10:05 UTC - Grafana shows incoming traffic jumped 5x in the last 10 minutes. Looks like the marketing promo email just went out. 
10:09 UTC - Site is sluggish/unresponsive. Checking Kubernetes. HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) is attempting to scale target deployments, but replica count is stuck.
10:14 UTC - Kube-scheduler shows multiple pods for `web-frontend` and `order-service` stuck in "Pending" state.
10:19 UTC - Checking node capacity. Cluster VM metrics look fine—CPU at 42%, Memory at 50%. There is plenty of bare metal headroom, but new pods are still refusing to schedule.
10:25 UTC - Attempting manual scale-up of `web-frontend` deployment. Replicas still won't spin up. Checking event logs: "0/12 nodes are available..." but no clear reason why. 
10:30 UTC - Paging Infra/Platform team. Database and cache layers are healthy, but app layer is starved and can't scale to meet the traffic. Still triaging scheduler issues.

description: A configuration change in the Seoul region incorrectly removed the setting that specifies the minimum healthy hosts for the EC2 DNS resolver fleet, so the system fell back to a very low default. The fleet's healthy host count dropped and in-VPC DNS queries from EC2 instances failed for ~84 minutes until capacity was manually restored. AWS added semantic config validation and per-hour throttling on host removal as remediations.
raw_input:
03:12 UTC - SEV-1 Alert: High rate of DNS resolution timeouts detected in ap-northeast-2. Multiple downstream services reporting database and API connection errors.

03:18 UTC - Internal Slack channels flooding. Core application servers failing target-group health checks because they cannot resolve internal VPC endpoints. Confirmed local VPC resolver (169.254.169.253) is timing out on queries.

03:30 UTC - Paging Networking/Route53 on-call. Testing from sandbox instance in Seoul: dig commands to both public and private zones are failing. Resolver fleet metrics show active host count has plummeted.

03:48 UTC - Confirmed resolver fleet size in ap-northeast-2 is extremely low. Unclear why auto-scaling isn't replacing unhealthy nodes or scaling up to meet demand. 

04:15 UTC - Network engineering team initiating manual override to provision additional resolver instances and force capacity back to baseline.

04:30 UTC - New resolver instances reporting healthy. DNS query success rates rising. Downstream service connection errors beginning to clear.

04:36 UTC - All core metrics stabilized. DNS latency back to normal in Seoul region. Declaring mitigation. Handing off to infra team to investigate why the fleet size dropped and remained low.

description: A deploy changed the type of a field in the PostgreSQL database used by the job-distribution service. Newly-written rows used the new type and old rows used the old type, so the distributor's strict schema validation failed on each scan and it stopped distributing work. Rolling back made the rows written between the two deploys unreadable, so distribution kept failing; recovery required hand-deploying a build that ignored the offending field, plus manual scaling.
raw_input:
14:15 UTC - ALERT: PagerDuty Sev-1 - JobDistributorQueueLengthSpike. Processing rate has dropped to 0/sec. Backlog is climbing rapidly.

14:18 UTC - Checking logs for job-distribution-service. Seeing heavy flood of validation errors: "Fatal: failing schema validation on row scan, aborting batch." Appears to be crashing loop on the main poll loop.

14:22 UTC - Checking change logs. Deploy `v2.44.1` finished at 14:08 UTC. Fits the timeline perfectly. Initiating rollback of job-distribution-service to `v2.44.0`.

14:31 UTC - Rollback to `v2.44.0` complete. However, error rate is still 100% and queue is still stalling. Logs still showing schema validation failures, but the error message format has slightly changed to "DeserializationError: unexpected field format".

14:38 UTC - Escalating to database-oncall and backend-platform-leads. It looks like the database state is corrupt or there are poison pills. Distributor is stuck on specific rows in the jobs table and refusing to proceed. 

14:48 UTC - DB-oncall confirms the database itself is healthy but we have a mix of unreadable records. Rolling back the service made things worse as it now can't parse rows written during the brief `v2.44.1` window.

14:58 UTC - Preparing emergency hotfix `v2.44.2-noop-field` to force the distributor to ignore the failing schema field entirely so it can bypass validation and unblock queue processing.

15:09 UTC - Hotfix deployed. Errors stopped. Queue processing has resumed, but backlog is at 450k jobs. Manually scaling distributor replicas from 4 to 20 to process the lag.

description: An IAM-role gap permitted out-of-band changes to AWS WAF outside of CircleCI's Terraform pipeline; an operator performing what they believed were read-only investigation actions modified WAF in a way that began blocking legitimate traffic to the `api.circleci.com` and `circleci.com` CloudFront distributions. Because the change wasn't recorded in Terraform, responders deprioritized WAF as a suspect and chased CORS errors and recent deploys until automated drift detection surfaced the discrepancy.
raw_input:
14:12 UTC - PagerDuty alert: High 5xx error rate on api.circleci.com. Traffic dropping ~40%.

14:18 UTC - Investigating recent deploys. No obvious regressions in main branch or recent commits. Reverting last deploy just in case.

14:35 UTC - Rollback complete, no change in error rate. Users reporting "Access Denied" and CORS errors on frontend. 

14:48 UTC - CloudFront metrics show significant spike in 403s. Checking WAF configurations via CLI, but look okay? Terraform shows no changes.

15:02 UTC - Suspecting a potential regional AWS networking issue or internal load balancer misconfiguration. Escalating to SRE lead.

15:20 UTC - CORS errors persist across all regions. Validating service mesh health, but nodes appear healthy.

15:45 UTC - Automated drift detection just triggered: AWS WAF state does not match Terraform definition. Something changed the WAF policy out-of-band. Investigating who/what touched the configuration.

description: A bad config (router rule) caused all of their edge routers to crash, taking down all of Cloudflare.
raw_input:
13:42 UTC - PagerDuty storm. Anycast IP space unreachable from multiple external probes. Global synthetic checks failing.
13:45 UTC - Edge ingress traffic drops to near 0. Internal monitoring losing telemetry from all edge PoPs. This is not regional.
13:49 UTC - Trying to SSH to SJC/LHR/NRT edge routers. High packet loss, then total connection timeout. Out-of-band (OOB) console connections also hanging.
13:54 UTC - Declaring Sev-1/P0. Spinning up incident bridge. Internal comms struggling, switching to backup Zoom.
13:58 UTC - Transit partners (Level3, Telia) reporting our BGP sessions are down/flapping globally. Edge routers seem completely dead or isolated.
14:04 UTC - Finally got console access on a router in SJC. Control plane CPU is at 100%, BGP daemon is unresponsive. Console barely accepting keystrokes.
14:10 UTC - Team looking at recent automated pipeline runs. Attempting hard power cycle of SJC edge router to see if it recovers or stays down.

description: During a maintenance of their private backbone network, an engineer made a typo in the Atlanta datacenter network configuration, causing all traffic coming from America and Europe flowing to this only datacenter, crushing it.
raw_input:
14:10 UTC - PagerDuty storm. Multiple alerts for ATL edge ingress capacity exceeding 95%. Gateway timeout rates (502/504) spiking for all AMER and EMEA users.

14:15 UTC - Traffic charts show massive, anomalous step-function spike in ingress traffic to ATL datacenter. EMEA and other AMER edge routers showing near-zero traffic.

14:22 UTC - ATL load balancers are OOMing and dropping packets under extreme load. Paged NetOps-Oncall. Investigating potential DDoS.

14:30 UTC - NetOps confirms no DDoS. Backbone traffic flows are totally misaligned. All WAN traffic is converging on ATL. Correlates with the backbone maintenance window that started at 14:00.

14:38 UTC - Tried to manually swing DNS/gslb to bypass ATL, but backbone routes are overriding. Internal services in ATL completely unresponsive.

14:45 UTC - NetOps identifying routing anomaly in ATL edge routers. Initiating emergency rollback of recent network configuration changes.

description: An incorrect ordering of the disabled BGP advertised prefixes caused malfunction on 19 datacenters.
raw_input:
13:47 UTC - PagerDuty storm. Global Anycast pingdom alerts firing. Heavy packet loss reported on edge.

13:51 UTC - Edge health checks failing for multiple regions. Confirmed 19 edge datacenters are completely unreachable via public internet. Internal backbone still up but edge is isolated.

13:56 UTC - External traceroutes to affected DCs are dying at upstream Tier 1 transits. Looks like massive routing withdrawal. Checking transit provider status pages - no global outages reported.

14:02 UTC - Netops escalated. BGP session monitoring shows prefix count dropped to 0 for the affected locations. Peers are not receiving our anycast routes.

14:08 UTC - Correlating with automated change management. Provisioning pipeline ran a routine prefix-list maintenance job at 13:45 UTC. 

14:15 UTC - Initiating emergency rollback of the prefix-list generation run and forcing manual push of last known good configuration to edge routers.

description: A change to our Tiered Cache system caused some requests to fail for users with status code 530. The impact lasted for almost six hours in total. We estimate that about 5% of all requests failed at peak. Because of the complexity of our system and a blind spot in our tests, we did not spot this when the change was released to our test environment.
raw_input:
13:12 UTC - PagerDuty: Edge-Gateway-5xx-Spike firing globally. Seeing about 1.5% increase in 530 errors.
13:30 UTC - Support escalating. Multiple customer reports of intermittent 530 errors. Edge logs show failures during internal proxy pass.
14:15 UTC - Peak error rate hitting ~5% global. Grafana dashboard showing 530s are highly concentrated on requests that query parent cache nodes. Origins healthy.
15:45 UTC - Traffic routing changes attempted to bypass impacted PoPs, but errors persist. This is an internal routing/cache issue, not external network. Escalating to @core-cache.
17:10 UTC - Identifying candidate release deployed earlier today. Preparing rollback of the edge proxy binary.
18:55 UTC - Rollback finished propagating globally. 530 errors back to baseline. Closing incident.

description: Several Cloudflare services became unavailable for 121 minutes on January 24, 2023 due to an error releasing code that manages service tokens. The incident degraded a wide range of Cloudflare products including aspects of our Workers platform, our Zero Trust solution, and control plane functions in our content delivery network (CDN).
raw_input:
16:14 UTC - PagerDuty storm. Alerts firing for Zero Trust authentication failures globally. Multiple internal alerts for API gateway 502/503 errors.
16:22 UTC - Dashboard is unresponsive for multiple tenants. Workers deployments failing with 500 Internal Server Error. Escalating to Sev-1.
16:45 UTC - Zero Trust users reporting being locked out of Access applications and WARP client failing to connect. Control plane functions (DNS updates, cache purges) are queuing up and timing out. Edge traffic routing is healthy, but anything requiring internal authorization is failing.
17:15 UTC - Incident response bridge active. Isolating the issue to the internal identity and token validation services. Seeing a massive spike in validation errors between internal microservices.
17:48 UTC - Investigating recent change logs. Database connections are stable but the internal auth API is systematically rejecting requests with authorization errors. Looking at active deploys in the last hour.
18:05 UTC - Initiating rollback of the latest deployment to the control plane management services.
18:12 UTC - Rollback in progress. Zero Trust authentication success rates are recovering. Workers API calls starting to succeed. Control plane backlog clearing.

description: On 4 October 2023, Cloudflare experienced DNS resolution problems starting at 07:00 UTC and ending at 11:00 UTC. Some users of 1.1.1.1 or products like WARP, Zero Trust, or third party DNS resolvers which use 1.1.1.1 may have received SERVFAIL DNS responses to valid queries. We’re very sorry for this outage. This outage was an internal software error and not the result of an attack. In this blog, we’re going to talk about what the failure was, why it occurred, and what we’re doing to make sure this doesn’t happen again.
raw_input:
07:05 UTC - PagerDuty storm. Anycast IP 1.1.1.1 reporting massive spike in SERVFAIL responses globally.
07:15 UTC - WARP and Zero Trust gateways reporting client connection drops. Internal probes to resolver cluster failing health checks.
07:45 UTC - Escalating to Edge DNS team. Internal test queries directly against recursive resolvers return SERVFAIL for valid external domains. Authoritative DNS path seems healthy.
08:20 UTC - Checking change logs. No active DNS configuration deployments in the last hour, but checking scheduled software release pipelines. Resolver CPU utilization is spiking.
09:10 UTC - Initiated traffic drains from heavily affected edge locations. Attempting rolling restart of the local resolver services.
10:15 UTC - SERVFAIL rates dropping in restarted zones. Pinning resolver daemon to the last-known stable release candidate while monitoring.
11:00 UTC - All regions recovered. Error rates back to nominal baseline. WARP and Zero Trust telemetry is fully green. Closing active incident, moving to postmortem.

description: On 18 Nov. 2025, a change in permissions in a database in Cloudflare's bot-detection systems caused a file to be output that exceeded the limits of the software that runs that system. That file was propagated througout Cloudflare's network, causing a systemwide outage.
raw_input:
11:12 UTC - PagerDuty storm. Global alerts for edge HTTP 502/504s. Anycast health checks failing on multiple PoPs.
11:18 UTC - Escalating to Sev-1. Edge service daemon is crashing in a loop on startup across all datacenters. OOM killer active.
11:25 UTC - Syslog on edge nodes showing "fatal: maximum buffer size exceeded" and "failed to parse ruleset" errors during daemon initialization.
11:34 UTC - Correlating crash timeline with global data sync. A signature update was propagated network-wide at 11:08 UTC.
11:42 UTC - Confirmed rule-file sync is distributed but edge nodes cannot ingest it. Engine fails to load the file, crashing the main traffic handler.
11:50 UTC - Attempting emergency bypass of the signature loader. Pushing manual override to force edge nodes to ignore the bad file.
11:58 UTC - Override applied. Daemon restarts succeeding, global traffic recovering.

description: A bad service discovery config in one of the clients brought down service discovery globally when a dependent client went down.
raw_input:
14:12 UTC - PagerDuty alert: Service Discovery API latency breaching 5s threshold (99th percentile).

14:15 - Monitoring dashboards showing 100% error rate for service resolution requests across all internal clusters.

14:18 - Multiple downstream microservices reporting "Connection Refused" and "DNS Resolution Failed" in logs.

14:22 - Escalating to Network/Infra team; suspecting potential backbone outage or global load balancer failure.

14:30 - Observed cascading failures: upstream application pods are crashing due to inability to locate dependencies.

14:45 - Attempted manual restart of primary Service Discovery control plane nodes; no change in system-wide error rates.

15:05 - Identifying anomalous request spikes originating from 'auth-client-svc'; isolating that specific client instance.

description: On January 15, 2022, at 9:00 AM ET, Tucows’ engineering team began planned maintenance work to migrate the Enom platform to a new cloud infrastructure. Due to the complexity of the cutover, the team encountered many issues resulting in continuous delays. The maintenance window was extended multiple times to address issues related to data replication, network routing, and DNS resolution issues impacting website accessibility and email delivery.
raw_input:
14:00 UTC - Maintenance window officially starts. Enom platform migration initiated. Traffic diverted, DBs set to read-only for final replication sync to new cloud env.
15:45 UTC - DB replication is crawling. Sync rate at 0.5 MB/s. Networking investigating potential bottleneck on the IPsec VPN tunnel between legacy DC and new cloud VPC.
17:00 UTC - Original maint window elapsed. Extending window by 3 hours. DB sync finally complete. Initiating network cutover and updating DNS/routing.
17:45 UTC - Automated alerts firing. Pingdom reporting Enom portal down (504 Gateway Timeout). Internal DNS servers failing health checks.
19:15 UTC - Escalation from support. Massive spike in tickets. Customers reporting custom domains using Enom nameservers are completely dark. DNS resolution failing globally. Email delivery bouncing with NXDOMAIN errors.
20:30 UTC - Paging network leads. Cloud frontends cannot reach backend database clusters. Routing table updates seem to be blackholing traffic. Attempting emergency rollback of DNS nameserver delegations, but changes are not propagating.
22:00 UTC - Status page updated. Maintenance window extended indefinitely. Still debugging routing mismatch and high packet loss between the transit gateway and legacy storage endpoints.

description: Sending multicast traffic without properly configuring switches caused an Etsy global outage.
raw_input:
11:04 UTC - PagerDuty storm. Global pingdom alerts firing. API and Web gateways reporting 504 timeouts.
11:08 UTC - Internal RPC latency spiking. Consul cluster reporting lost quorum, nodes flapping. SSH to prod-web-01 times out.
11:15 UTC - Network switch alerts firing. Core switches in DC1 and DC2 reporting 100% CPU utilization. Severe packet loss (>80%) on internal VLANs.
11:22 UTC - Escalated to Network Engineering. Appears to be an internal network storm or loop. Web traffic dropped to near-zero.
11:31 UTC - DB connections dropping, replica lag alerting. Tried isolating DC1 rack switches, but control plane is unresponsive.
11:40 UTC - Still unable to access switch CLIs due to high CPU. Initiating emergency rollback of the last config change and app deploys from 11:00 UTC.

description: Configuration changes to Facebook's backbone routers caused a global outage of all Facebook properties and internal tools.
raw_input:
15:40 UTC - Multiple PagerDuty alerts firing. Internal DNS resolution failures. External health checks for facebook.com, instagram.com, and whatsapp.net failing globally.

15:45 UTC - VPN dropped. Unable to re-authenticate. Workplace is down. Moving incident coordination to emergency external Slack channel.

15:52 UTC - External network probes show complete loss of BGP routes for our primary ASNs. It looks like our DNS authoritative name servers are completely unreachable from the public internet.

16:05 UTC - Trying to access edge routers via out-of-band (OOB) management console. Connections are timing out. We have no remote visibility into the network backbone.

16:18 UTC - Total internal blackout. Teams are reporting they cannot badge into physical buildings or conference rooms. Local office networks are down.

16:35 UTC - Dispatched on-call net-eng physical response team to the Santa Clara data center to attempt local serial console access.

description: A bad config took down both Facebook and Instagram.
raw_input:
15:40 UTC - ALERT: Edge DNS resolution failure rate >90% for facebook.com and instagram.com. Egress traffic dropping off a cliff.
15:45 UTC - PagerDuty storm. Internal tooling starting to go dark. VPN connections dropping for on-call engineers.
15:55 UTC - Confirmed total blackout of FB and IG web/app clients globally. Cannot SSH into any production clusters. Internal communication channels down; migrating incident response to OOB Signal group.
16:10 UTC - Netops reports global BGP routes for our ASNs have withdrawn. External Looking Glass servers show 0 active routes.
16:30 UTC - Attempting to access core routers via OOB console lines. Connections failing/timing out. We are completely locked out of the network remotely.
16:50 UTC - Site Ops dispatched to nearest physical datacenters to attempt direct serial connection to backbone routers.

description: On January 13th, 2022, a specific code path in Firefox's network stack triggered a problem in the HTTP/3 protocol implementation. This blocked network communication and made Firefox unresponsive, unable to load web content for nearly two hours.
raw_input:
07:28 UTC - Automated alert: Massive drop in telemetry ping volume globally. Checking cloud-platform status pages for widespread AWS/GCP outages. None found.

07:38 UTC - Bugzilla getting flooded with duplicate bugs. Users reporting Firefox suddenly cannot load any websites. Symptoms: infinite spinner, blank pages, tab crashes. Affects latest Release build.

07:52 UTC - On-call repro: clean profile on Firefox Release hangs on any page load. UI itself is responsive, but network layer seems completely dead. Looking at about:support, socket thread appears blocked.

08:12 UTC - Captured local NSPR logs. Socket thread is indeed spinning or deadlocked. What changed? No client update was pushed today. Must be a remote service or network payload triggering this behavior on startup.

08:27 UTC - Internal testing shows blocking outgoing traffic to our settings/telemetry servers allows pages to load. Appears to be triggered by an external service response. Disabling DNS-over-HTTPS doesn't help.

08:42 UTC - Breakthrough: Turning off HTTP/3 in about:config (network.http.http3 = false) instantly resolves the issue and pages load normally. 

08:55 UTC - Deploying a remote settings block to disable HTTP/3 globally for all clients via the push service. Monitoring telemetry ingress for recovery.

description: A Puppet manifest bug restarted only the authoritative nameserver (not the caching one) after an IP change, causing query timeouts. The deploy run during incident response then rebuilt the zone file from an internal provisioning API call that itself depended on DNS, producing a corrupt zone with `NXDOMAIN` for many records. Memory exhaustion from spawned processes on the fileservers extended impact to 1h35m of partial downtime.
raw_input:
10:15 UTC - PagerDuty storm: `DNS_Query_Timeout` alerts firing globally. Internal apps reporting connection errors to database and external APIs.

10:23 UTC - Confirmed authoritative DNS is dropping packets. `dig @ns1.ourdomain.com` is timing out. Caching DNS servers are responding but TTLs are starting to expire. Latency spiking.

10:35 UTC - Triggered emergency zone file rebuild and config redeploy to force-update IP routing on the nameservers.

10:42 UTC - Deploy finished but queries are now returning `NXDOMAIN` for core production records (api, web, db). Major outage declared. Sev-1 incident channel opened.

10:55 UTC - Fileservers (fs-01 through fs-08) alerting on extreme load and memory exhaustion. OOM killer active. Mass client reconnects/spawned processes suspected.

11:20 UTC - Manually rolling back zone configurations on ns1 and ns2. Restoring static backup zone files.

11:35 UTC - Core DNS resolution recovered. Manually recycling apache/gunicorn processes on fileservers to clear memory bloat.

11:50 UTC - Fileserver load stabilized. Error rates back to baseline. Monitoring closely.

description: A configuration change rolled out to GitHub.com databases broke the way hosts answered routing-service health-check pings, so the production read-only endpoint was marked unhealthy and inaccessible. With reads broken, the entire site was down for read operations for 36 minutes until the change was reverted.
raw_input:
18:12 UTC - PagerDuty spike. Web frontends reporting massive 503 surge on read paths. Users reporting "No healthy upstream" on site.
18:16 UTC - Internal status page shows database read-replicas cluster health dropping to 0%. Routing tier is marking all read-only DB hosts as unhealthy.
18:21 UTC - Logged into db-replica-01. Host is up, load average normal, DB process running and accepting queries locally. Health-check daemon on port 8000 returning errors to HTTP queries.
18:29 UTC - On-call DB infra paged. Confirmed routing layer is dropping all read replica nodes from the active pool due to failed health pings.
18:35 UTC - Correlating incident start with recent automated host configuration rollout at 18:10. Initiating immediate emergency rollback of the DB host configuration update.
18:42 UTC - Rollback finished. DB health check endpoints returning 200 OK again. Hosts are gradually being marked healthy by the routing tier.
18:48 UTC - Traffic recovering. Read-only DB pool size back to 100%. Site error rates returning to normal. Closing out active mitigation.

description: A telemetry gap caused security policies to be auto-applied to backend storage accounts in GitHub's underlying compute provider, blocking access to critical VM metadata. All VM create/delete/reimage operations failed, taking out Actions hosted runners in every region, Codespaces, Copilot coding agent, CodeQL, Dependabot, GitHub Enterprise Importer, and Pages for ~5h53m.
raw_input:
08:12 UTC - PagerDuty storm. Actions runner allocation success rate dropped to 0% globally. Queue times spiking. SEV-1 declared.
08:25 UTC - Reports coming in that Codespaces starts are failing with provisioning timeouts. Copilot telemetry showing massive spike in 503s/timeouts. Pages builds stuck in "Queued".
08:50 UTC - Compute orchestrator logs flooded with VM creation and reimage failures across all subscription pools. Error: "ComputeProviderError: VM provisioning failed during customization phase".
09:35 UTC - Cloud provider status page is green, but our internal hypervisor/compute API calls are timing out. Specifically, newly spun-up VMs are failing health checks and cannot pull metadata.
10:45 UTC - Network and Security teams paged in. Investigating if this is a routing/firewall issue or an IAM/permission block on the compute host storage endpoints.
11:30 UTC - Verified that existing VMs are running but cannot be rotated/reimaged. Any operation requiring VM deletion or creation is hard-failing. Broad impact confirmed across Actions, Dependabot, CodeQL, and Pages.
13:15 UTC - Focus is on why VM boot-strapping agent cannot access the orchestrator's metadata endpoints. Network engineering attempting to trace the block.

description: A bad config combined with an uncommon set of failures led to an outage of a database cluster, taking the API and Dashboard offline.
raw_input:
11:14 UTC - PagerDuty alert: API 5xx error rate > 5% on prod-api-gateway.
11:18 UTC - Pingdom alerting on main Dashboard (HTTP 504 Gateway Timeout). Internal Slack #ops starting to light up with customer reports.
11:24 UTC - App logs show Database Connection Timeout errors. Checking DB metrics, CPU on db-primary is at 100% and IOPS pegged.
11:32 UTC - db-primary is now completely unresponsive. Automated failover to db-replica-01 initiated by orchestrator.
11:38 UTC - Failover failed. db-replica-01 went into a crash loop immediately after promoting. db-replica-02 is also refusing connections.
11:45 UTC - Escalating to Database Infrastructure team. Attempting manual bootstrap of the cluster, but nodes are refusing to form a quorum. API and Dashboard remain completely offline.

description: Initial GCVE provisioning was performed with a legacy option, which lead to a 'fixed term' contract with automatic deletion at the end of that period.
raw_input:
08:12 UTC - PagerDuty storm. Multiple core production services reporting connection timeouts. Uptime checks failing for all workloads on GCVE.
08:17 UTC - Verified network path. Interconnect is up, but cannot ping GCVE vCenter or NSX-T endpoints. Port 443 timed out.
08:25 UTC - Logged into GCP Console. GCVE portal is completely empty. Private Cloud "us-east1-pc" is not listed. No resources visible.
08:31 UTC - Checking Cloud Audit Logs for IAM deletions. Filter: protoPayload.serviceName="vmwareengine.googleapis.com" and protoPayload.methodName:"delete". Zero results. No user initiated a delete.
08:40 UTC - Opened P1 Support Case with Google (Case #823910). Escalated to TAM.
08:52 UTC - Internal sync: Confirmed with infra team that no decommissioning work or migrations were scheduled. VMs are completely dark, storage unreachable.
09:05 UTC - Google Support acknowledges. State on their end shows the private cloud status as "DELETED" but they are investigating why it triggered without user intervention.

description: A bad config (autogenerated) removed all Google Compute Engine IP blocks from BGP announcements.
raw_input:
08:14 UTC - PagerDuty: Major outage alert. Multiple external uptime checks (pingdom, synthetic probes) failing globally for all GCE-hosted services.

08:17 UTC - Support channels lighting up. Users reporting connection timeouts. Checked edge traffic dashboards, ingress traffic has dropped to absolute zero.

08:23 UTC - Attempting to SSH into bastion hosts via public IPs - connection timeout. Serial console on GCE instances shows VMs are up and running, but no external network connectivity.

08:30 UTC - CDN edge nodes reporting 100% gateway timeouts (504s) trying to reach origin backends. Internal VPC-to-VPC traffic seems operational, but any public egress/ingress is blackholing.

08:39 UTC - Running traceroute from external network. Packets drop immediately after hitting upstream ISP borders. Looking glass servers show our ASN is not advertising any of our primary GCE public IP blocks.

08:45 UTC - Declaring P1 incident. Paging NetOps and Infra-Oncall. Checking recent CI/CD deployments to see if any network or DNS changes went out.

description: A bad config (autogenerated) took down most Google services.
raw_input:
11:45 UTC - PagerDuty storm. Multiple SEV1 alerts for Gmail, YouTube, and GCP Console. High HTTP 5xx rates globally.
11:50 UTC - Trying to access internal dashboards. Keep getting redirected to SSO login which is failing with 500 Internal Server Error.
11:55 UTC - Decoupled on-call bridge established. Internal chat is down, spinning up backup external Slack. Declaring SEV0.

12:10 UTC - Attempting manual rollbacks of last cluster pushes, but deployment pipelines are failing authentication checks.
12:18 UTC - Checking dynamic system state. SRE teams isolating database provisioning services to see why storage quota is reporting zero.

description: A bad config caused a quota service to fail, which caused multiple services to fail (including gmail).
raw_input:
14:12 UTC - PagerDuty storm. High priority alert: Gmail API elevated 5xx error rates (currently 12% and climbing). Also seeing latency spikes on user-settings and storage-metadata services.

14:17 UTC - External reports of Gmail 502 errors flowing in. Incident bridge opened. Dashboard shows cascading failures across multiple product suites, not just Gmail.

14:23 UTC - Frontend services are healthy but downstream RPCs are timing out. Traces show requests stalling at the authentication/authorization gates.

14:31 UTC - Isolated common dependency: `quota-enforcer-service` is throwing 100% Deadlines Exceeded. It seems to be rejecting all validation requests, blocking Gmail mail delivery and Drive writes.

14:45 UTC - Quota team on-call is online. They report quota-enforcer service is stuck in a boot loop across all clusters after a scheduled rolling restart. Investigating logs for why it won't bind.

description: `/` was checked into the URL blacklist, causing every URL to show a warning.
raw_input:
18:14 UTC - PagerDuty alert: High rate of 'Unsafe Site' warnings served globally. Traffic to target pages dropping.
18:18 UTC - Support channels flooding with reports. Users seeing "This URL has been blocked" warning for google.com, major news sites, and even our own homepage.
18:25 UTC - Confirmed bypass works if we disable the URL filter locally. Looks like the URL Reputation Service is returning 'malicious' match for literally every outbound link.
18:32 UTC - Paged Security Engine on-call. Reputation API is healthy but showing a 100% block rate. Checking database replication and recent rule updates.
18:41 UTC - Initiating immediate rollback of the reputation list update pushed at 18:05 UTC to see if it clears the false positives.
18:49 UTC - Rollback completed. Block rates dropping back to normal baseline. Users reporting normal access restored.

description: A bug in configuration roll-out to a load balancer lead to increased error rates for 22 minutes.
raw_input:

description: A bug in third-party datacenter control system code caused excessive interactions during a control-host failover, making the cooling control system unresponsive. Most of the datacenter correctly failed cooling into "max cooling" mode, but in a small portion the cooling units shut down instead, and the operator-initiated "purge" mode also failed because the PLCs controlling the air handlers had become unresponsive too. EC2 servers in one Tokyo AZ overheated and powered off; customers using ALB + AWS WAF or sticky sessions saw cross-AZ impact despite running multi-AZ.

14:12 UTC - PagerDuty alert: Critical temperature threshold exceeded in Tokyo-1 AZ. Multiple rack-level thermal alerts triggering simultaneously.

14:18 - NOC reports loss of telemetry from core datacenter cooling controllers. Manual "Purge" command issued via BMS console returning "Request Timeout" errors.

14:24 - Massive spike in EC2 health check failures in Tokyo-1. ALB error rates climbing; ELB logs showing 5xxs for services pinned to this AZ.

14:31 - Infrastructure dashboard showing "Non-Responsive" state for localized PLC controllers. Cooling units in sector 4-B have entered an unexpected "OFF" state rather than "FAIL-SAFE/MAX."

14:45 - Engineering team escalated. Incident Response initiated. Cross-AZ traffic patterns showing increased latency/dropped connections for sticky-session users as workloads shift and saturate remaining healthy AZ capacity.

14:58 - Confirming physical hardware thermal shutdowns in racks 12 through 19. Powering down unresponsive control hosts to attempt manual hardware override.

15:15 - Current status: Cooling system remains unresponsive to control software. Mitigation focused on force-draining all traffic from Tokyo-1 AZ while onsite team prepares manual override of AHU breakers.

description: A malfunctioning IMP ([Interface Message Processor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interface_Message_Processor)) corrupted routing data, software recomputed checksums propagating bad data with good checksums, incorrect sequence numbers caused buffers to fill, full buffers caused loss of keepalive packets and nodes took themselves off the network. From 1980.
raw_input:

10:18 - Escalating to NOC. Multiple IMPs reporting "Buffer Full" status across the backbone.

10:22 - Node 9 unreachable. Traffic latency spiking to 3000ms on remaining segments. 

10:28 - Remote diagnostics showing recurring checksum errors on IMP 4 interface; software reporting "Invalid Routing Sequence" alerts.

10:35 - Massive churn in the routing table. IMP 4 is flapping; broadcasting corrupt routing updates to neighbors.

10:42 - IMP 4, 7, 9, 12, and 15 have triggered automatic self-isolation. Network visibility significantly degraded.

10:50 - Core transit nodes unable to establish keepalives. Total segment collapse. Paging systems engineering lead.

description: A latent race condition in DynamoDB's DNS management left the regional endpoint `dynamodb.us-east-1.amazonaws.com` with an empty record. Two redundant "DNS Enactor" processes raced when one was unusually delayed; a second Enactor applied a newer plan and ran cleanup while the first overwrote the regional endpoint with a stale older plan, which the cleanup process then deleted. The DynamoDB outage cascaded into EC2 (DWFM "congestive collapse"), Network Load Balancer health-check flapping, and Lambda for ~15 hours.

description: During an aggregation-switch ISSU upgrade, terminating an agent on one switch left the link up just long enough for the peer's MLAG failover to use the disruptive (rather than stateful) path, freezing the network for ~90 seconds. That spike caused fileserver Pacemaker/Heartbeat/DRBD pairs in different racks to exceed their heartbeats and STONITH each other, leaving many active/passive pairs with both nodes powered off; recovery required identifying the previously-active node from logs for each pair and took >5 hours.

description: Customers noticed their per-organization `INC-N` IDs jumping by exactly 32 (e.g. `#INC-7` directly to `#INC-39`) after a Postgres HA upgrade that promoted a follower to primary. Postgres's `nextval` pre-allocates `SEQ_LOG_VALS = 32` sequence values on the WAL to avoid logging every `nextval`; a follower sees the post-crash state, so when promoted the sequence jumps forward by up to 32. Fix was to replace `nextval` with a `SELECT MAX(external_id)+1` trigger.

description: Certificates that were valid for one year were created. Instead of using an appropriate library, someone wrote code that computed one year to be the current date plus one year. On February 29th 2012, this resulted in the creation of certificates with an expiration date of February 29th 2013, which were rejected because of the invalid date. This caused an Azure global outage that lasted for most of a day.

description: Backwards time flow from tracking [the 27th leap second on 2016-12-31T23:59:60Z](https://hpiers.obspm.fr/iers/bul/bulc/bulletinc.52) caused the weighted round-robin selection of DNS resolvers (RRDNS) to panic and fail on some CNAME lookups.  Go's `time.Now()` was incorrectly assumed to be monotonic; this injected negative values into calls to `rand.Int63n()`, which panics in that case.

00:17 UTC - Observed logs show panic occurring inside `rand.Int63n` calls. Digging into stack trace; looking at how we seed the PRNG.

00:25 UTC - Traffic stabilized on the backup pool. Primary pool still crashing immediately upon restart. Escalating to core runtime team.

description: Leap second code was called from the timer interrupt handler, which held `xtime_lock`. That code did a `printk` to log the leap second. `printk` wakes up `klogd`, which can sometimes try to get the time, which waits on `xtime_lock`, causing a deadlock.

08:43 UTC - SSH connection to node-prod-04 failing. Console output shows "NMI watchdog: BUG: soft lockup - CPU#2 stuck for 23s!"
08:46 UTC - Monitoring dashboard shows total request drop for region us-east-1. Multiple nodes reporting "load average" > 100.
08:50 UTC - Attempted manual reboot of node-prod-04 via IPMI; node unresponsive.
08:55 UTC - Escalation to Kernel Engineering team. Preliminary dmesg snippet shows backtrace stuck in `xtime_lock` while spinning on `printk`.
09:02 UTC - Cluster-wide panic appearing across 40% of the fleet. Disabling automated traffic shifting to prevent cascading failure.
09:15 UTC - Incident declared. All nodes exhibiting identical stack traces involving interrupt handlers and printk calls.

description: When a leap second occurred, `CLOCK_REALTIME` was rewound by one second. This was not done via a mechanism that would update `hrtimer base.offset`. This meant that when a timer interrupt happened, TIMER_ABSTIME CLOCK_REALTIME timers got expired one second early, including timers set for less than one second. This caused applications that used sleep for less than one second in a loop to spinwait without sleeping, causing high load on many systems. This caused a large number of web services to go down in 2012.
raw_input:

00:00:02 UTC - Alerts firing: High latency on load balancers. 503 errors spiking globally.

00:01:15 UTC - Confirming this is cross-service. Not an application deployment issue. Possible underlying infrastructure sync problem.

description: Most Firefox add-ons stopped working around May 4th 2019 when a certificate expired. Firefox requires a valid certificate chain to prevent malware. About nine hours later, Mozilla pushed a privileged add-on that injected a valid certificate into Firefox's certificate store, creating a valid chain and unblocking add-ons. This disabled effectively all add-ons, about 15,000, and the resolution took approximately 15-21 hours for most users. Some user data was lost. Previously Mozilla [posted](https://web.archive.org/web/20250408175304/https://hacks.mozilla.org/2019/05/technical-details-on-the-recent-firefox-add-on-outage/) about the technical details.
raw_input:
08:15 UTC - Spike in incoming support tickets: users reporting all installed add-ons are "disabled" or "could not be verified."

08:42 UTC - Alert: Firefox telemetry showing massive drop in active add-on sessions across all release channels. Dashboard showing near-zero success rate for add-on signature verification checks.

09:10 UTC - Engineering investigation initiated. Initial theory: potential outage on the signing infrastructure (signing.telemetry.mozilla.org). 

10:05 UTC - Confirmed: Signing services are responding correctly, yet clients are rejecting all existing add-on certificates. No recent deployment to signing infra.

11:30 UTC - Escalation to Release Engineering. Frontend/UI teams reporting that local browser consoles are throwing "CERT_EXPIRED" errors on every installed extension.

13:45 UTC - Decision to trigger emergency hotfix via Studies/Telemetry rollout. Pushing a remote configuration to force-bypass certificate checks for known IDs.

16:20 UTC - Deployment of hotfix underway. Monitoring progress: some users reporting add-ons returning, but high volume of reports indicating local data/settings for extensions have been purged during the disablement event.

18:00 UTC - Global rollout of hotfix ongoing. High latency in propagation; many users still seeing disabled status. Investigating why some users are not receiving the pushed certificate update.

description: At peak Wednesday-afternoon load, the primary database backed up with queued operations to the point that it stopped catching up; rolling back recent changes had no isolated effect because the queue depth had already saturated the system, and a primary failover to kill queued ops only bought temporary headroom. After the runnable-queue was drained, builds were stuck in the prior queue stage; manually promoting them flooded the next queue, and the build-scheduler's failure-mode throttles fired on what were actually normal conditions and backed off precisely when more throughput was needed. CircleCI rebuilt tooling on the fly to clear a 17-hour backlog.

description: A blue/green upgrade of the workflows database succeeded mechanically, but the post-cutover database was running every query against disk because its statistics tables had not been updated. The team ran `ANALYZE` early in the upgrade procedure, but a second major-version upgrade in the same deployment then made those statistics stale, leaving the planner without usable indexes after the cutover. Workflows latency spiked, jobs were dropped after exhausting their 10-minute retry, and the team eventually re-promoted the old (blue) database to recover.

description: The GitHub platform encountered a novel failure mode when processing a schema migration on a large MySQL table. Schema migrations are a common task at GitHub and often take weeks to complete. The final step in a migration is to perform a rename to move the updated table into the correct place. During the final step of this migration a significant portion of our MySQL read replicas entered a semaphore deadlock. Our MySQL clusters consist of a primary node for write traffic, multiple read replicas for production traffic, and several replicas that serve internal read traffic for backup and analytics purposes. The read replicas that hit the deadlock entered a crash-recovery state causing an increased load on healthy read replicas. Due to the cascading nature of this scenario, there were not enough active read replicas to handle production requests which impacted the availability of core GitHub services.

description: Two weeks of intermittent app timeouts, with traces showing requests waiting up to 20s for a connection from Go's `database/sql` pool, but contention spread across many endpoints rather than a single slow query. After 24 deploys' worth of fixes (materialized views, indexes, lock timeouts, async Slack-webhook handling, and a custom `ngrok/sqlmw` middleware to attribute connection-pool hold time per operation), the root cause turned out to be an unnecessary transaction wrapping every Slack modal submission — many small fast transactions were in aggregate exhausting the pool.

description: Human error. On February 28th 2017 9:37AM PST, the Amazon S3 team was debugging a minor issue. Despite using an established playbook, one of the commands intending to remove a small number of servers was issued with a typo, inadvertently causing a larger set of servers to be removed. These servers supported critical S3 systems. As a result, dependent systems required a full restart to correctly operate, and the system underwent widespread outages for US-EAST-1 (Northern Virginia) until final resolution at 1:54PM PST. Since Amazon's own services such as EC2 and EBS rely on S3 as well, it caused a vast cascading failure which affected hundreds of companies.

10:45 PST - Investigating connectivity issues between S3 indices and dependent subsystems. Significant thread exhaustion observed on service nodes; initiating cluster-wide restart procedures to clear stuck states.

11:30 PST - System recovery ongoing. Bootstrapping indices for US-EAST-1 is extremely slow due to the sheer volume of dependent requests flooding the re-initializing partitions.

13:10 PST - Traffic beginning to stabilize. Error rates dropping; S3 PUT/GET success metrics slowly recovering toward baseline.

description: A Kinesis cell newly migrated to a new architecture had an unusually high number of very low-throughput shards. The cell management system distributed the shards unevenly, so a few hosts ended up handling huge numbers of shards and produced abnormally large status messages. The system misinterpreted slow status messages as host failures, kicked off a redistribution storm, and overloaded the secure-connection provisioning subsystem. CloudWatch Logs, S3 event notifications, Firehose, ECS, Lambda, Redshift, MWAA, and Glue all degraded for ~7 hours.
raw_input:
14:12 UTC - PagerDuty alert: Kinesis cell 'us-east-1-shard-manager' threshold exceeded. High CPU usage on 4 internal management nodes.

14:28 UTC - Monitoring dashboard shows rapid spike in host heartbeat failures across the new cell.

14:45 UTC - Received multiple alerts for "Secure Connection Provisioning" latency. Escalating to Network/Security team as potential DDoS.

15:10 UTC - Kinesis throughput dropping. Error rates rising in Firehose and S3 event triggers. ECS service tasks failing to scale.

15:40 UTC - Incident declared. AWS internal status page showing cascading degradation across Glue, MWAA, and Redshift clusters.

16:20 UTC - Logs showing "Timeout waiting for status message" on shard management hosts. Attempting to restart individual shards to relieve pressure.

17:05 UTC - Redistribution storm detected. Shard manager continuously shuffling tasks; node CPU remains at 100%.

19:30 UTC - Service-wide degradation persists. Throttling all non-critical Kinesis API traffic to attempt stabilization.

description: A double free revealed by a database update caused all "impression bus" servers to crash simultaneously. This wasn't caught in staging and made it into production because a time delay is required to trigger the bug, and the staging period didn't have a built-in delay.

09:12 UTC - PagerDuty: Critical alert, "Impression Bus Cluster Down." 0% health across all instances in us-east-1.
09:14 - Dashboards show 100% 5xx error rate on the ad-serving frontend. Traffic is hitting a wall.
09:18 - Attempting manual restart of the primary cluster. Service comes up for 10 seconds then immediately crashes again.
09:22 - Checking logs on individual nodes. Seeing "Segmentation fault" and "Double free or corruption" traces repeating across all instances.
09:28 - Traffic diverted to standby region. Standby cluster is now showing the same symptoms—crashing seconds after start.
09:35 - Escalating to Database Reliability team; looking for correlation with the latest schema update pushed at 09:10.
09:42 - Confirmed: All nodes in the cluster are entering a boot loop. No logs indicating external network issues, just local memory corruption.
09:55 - Initiating rollback to the previous deployment version to stop the crash loop.

description: A bad line of C code introduced a race hazard which in due course collapsed the phone network. After a planned outage, the quickfire resumption messages triggered the race,  causing more reboots which retriggered the problem. "The problem repeated iteratively throughout the 114 switches in the network, blocking over 50 million calls in the nine hours it took to stabilize the system." From 1990.

08:18 UTC - Escalating to NOC. Seeing cascade of "REBOOT_REQUIRED" traps across switches 04-09.
08:45 UTC - Network congestion critical. Calls failing in the thousands. Attempting manual restart of the primary control bus.
09:20 UTC - Control bus restart completed. System stable for 5 minutes, then sudden spike in reboots across cluster 12.
10:05 UTC - Pattern identified: switch resets triggering secondary failures in neighboring hardware. Total outage expanding to 40+ switches.
12:30 UTC - 50M calls blocked. Attempting to throttle resumption signal velocity, but nodes are auto-restarting faster than we can intervene.
15:45 UTC - Hardware isolation procedures failing. Every attempt to bring nodes back online results in immediate kernel panic/reboot loops.
17:10 UTC - Implemented manual site-by-site bypass. Network beginning to stabilize, but load remains erratic. Investigating potential memory corruption.

description: On Tuesday, April 5th, 2022, starting at 7:38 UTC, 775 Atlassian customers lost access to their Atlassian products. 10:30 UTC - Determining restoration strategy; manual site-by-site recovery required due to complex data consistency issues.
12:00 UTC - Communication team notified; drafting external messaging for affected customers as restoration timeline remains unclear.

description: Basecamp's network was under a DDoS attack during a 100-minute window on March 24, 2014. Same-day announcement that the attack was in progress: [signalvnoise.com](https://signalvnoise.com/posts/3728-basecamp-was-under-network-attack-this-morning).

description: In November 2018 a database hit the integer limit, leaving the service in read-only mode. Same-day status update during the incident: [signalvnoise.com](https://web.archive.org/web/20220530044506/https://m.signalvnoise.com/update-on-basecamp-3-being-stuck-in-read-only-as-of-nov-8-922am-cst/).

09:18 UTC - Internal dashboard shows site is operational but users reporting "Read-only mode" banners and failures when attempting to save or update content.

09:25 UTC - SSH'd into primary db. `SHOW PROCESSLIST` shows connections hanging. Check disk space and memory: both look healthy.

09:35 UTC - Attempted manual row insert on a core table: "General error: 1467 Failed to read auto-increment value from storage engine."

09:42 UTC - Escalated to SRE Lead. Investigating potential index corruption or lock contention. Queries failing even with superuser privileges.

09:55 UTC - Confirmed all write attempts result in identical error across shards. Load is low, but write failure rate is 100%.

10:10 UTC - Initiated emergency maintenance window. Setting service status page to "Degraded - Read Only" while we triage the auto-increment failure.

description: In July 2014, BBC Online experienced a very long outage of several of its popular online services including the BBC iPlayer. When the database backend was overloaded, it had started to throttle requests from various services. Services that hadn't cached the database responses locally began timing out and eventually failed completely.

14:15 UTC - Users reporting "Service Unavailable" errors on iPlayer front-end. Internal service health checks starting to flap.

14:22 UTC - Error rates spiking across core API services. Database CPU utilization pegged at 98%.

14:30 UTC - Escalating to DB Infrastructure team. Rolling restart of load balancers attempted; no improvement in traffic shedding.

14:45 UTC - Full outage confirmed for iPlayer and several regional news sub-services. App servers showing massive thread exhaustion due to connection timeouts.

15:05 UTC - Initiated emergency traffic throttling at the edge. Database load remains saturated despite dropped requests.

15:20 UTC - Investigating potential circular dependency: app service retries are overwhelming DB connection pools during timeout periods.

description: In July 2017 several malicious Maven packages were included in JCenter with an impersonation attack. Those packages lived in JCenter for over a year and supposedly affected several Android apps that resulted in having malware code injected by those dependencies from JCenter.

15:30 - Incident escalated to Security Team. We are seeing intermittent runtime crashes in the staging environment. Stack traces point to obfuscated code paths in the newly pulled dependencies.

15:55 - Global lock on all external Maven repository syncs. Forcing rollbacks to known-good cached dependency snapshots.

16:20 - Confirmed: The affected builds contain unexpected dex files being injected at compile time. Investigating if this is a registry compromise or a supply chain attack on specific upstream packages.

description: By January 4, 2023, our internal investigation had determined the scope of the intrusion by the unauthorized third party and the entry path of the attack. To date, we have learned that an unauthorized third party leveraged malware deployed to a CircleCI engineer’s laptop in order to steal a valid, 2FA-backed SSO session. This machine was compromised on December 16, 2022. The malware was not detected by our antivirus software. Our investigation indicates that the malware was able to execute session cookie theft, enabling them to impersonate the targeted employee in a remote location and then escalate access to a subset of our production systems.
raw_input:
16:04 UTC - PagerDuty alert: Unusual spike in unauthorized access attempts detected on production internal dashboard.

16:12 UTC - Security team reports a series of successful logins from unrecognized IP ranges for an internal engineer account, despite 2FA being active.

16:25 UTC - System logs indicate anomalous behavior: production configuration changes being triggered by an internal admin session; initiating session revocation for affected user.

16:40 UTC - Incident Command established. Escalating to SRE and Security Leads. Access patterns still persisting despite password resets.

17:05 UTC - Confirmed lateral movement across multiple staging and production environments. Implementing broad restrictive firewall rules for all internal admin credentials.

17:22 UTC - Discovered unauthorized session tokens being used to bypass SSO challenges. Rotating all active global session cookies and forcing re-authentication for all staff.

18:00 UTC - Access patterns stabilized. Investigating source of initial credential compromise; user machine being isolated for forensic imaging.

description: CircleCI's database provider, MongoHQ, was breached on October 27, 2013, and CircleCI's MongoDB was among the databases accessed; CircleCI was holding GitHub OAuth tokens, Heroku API tokens, AWS IAM keys, and SSH deploy/user keys for customers in that database. On notification, CircleCI shut down the site and all builds, then worked with GitHub, Heroku, and AWS to revoke every OAuth token, API token, IAM key, and SSH key it had handed out, and cycled all of its own keys and caches.
raw_input:
10:15 UTC - PagerDuty alert: Critical threshold reached for database latency. Spike in 500 errors across main application dashboard.

10:22 - Confirmed: Web UI is non-responsive. Users reporting "Database Connection Error" intermittently. 

10:30 - Multiple reports from internal monitoring showing unauthorized connection attempts to the production MongoDB cluster.

10:45 - Emergency meeting convened. Decision made to pull the plug: taking the site offline immediately to prevent further data exposure.

11:00 - Site is fully down. All CI builds paused. Investigating integrity of user credentials stored in the DB.

11:20 - Confirming high-risk data (GitHub tokens, AWS IAM keys, SSH keys) may be compromised. Escalating to engineering leads.

11:45 - Initiating full revocation process with AWS, GitHub, and Heroku. Drafting site-wide security notice.

description: Slow queries on the MongoDB replica sets backing the build queue caused workflows to back up over a two-week run of incidents. A roughly concurrent minor-version JVM upgrade enabled Docker-awareness by default, which silently shrank thread and connection pools across most JVM services and constrained throughput, masking the underlying MongoDB capacity problem. Tuning thread/connection pools and upsizing MongoDB stabilized the platform after multiple cascading outages on March 26, April 2, April 3, and April 10.
raw_input:
14:15 UTC - PagerDuty alert: Workflow processing latency exceeding 500s threshold. Queue depth spiked from 2k to 45k in 10 minutes.

14:22 UTC - Grafana dashboard shows 90% utilization on MongoDB replica set primary. High number of blocked threads across all JVM services.

14:35 UTC - Investigating potential network partition. DB connection pool exhaustion warnings appearing in service logs. Restarting two instances of the build-queue service temporarily clears backlog.

15:02 UTC - Backlog returning. Latency spikes again. Confirmed no recent code deployments, though JVM versions were bumped across the cluster two weeks ago. 

15:20 UTC - Cascading failure. API gateway timing out waiting for build-queue service. Marking service as degraded in Statuspage.

15:45 UTC - Thread dumps show massive contention in the JVM services, but CPU usage is lower than expected. Adding capacity to the DB cluster to see if it alleviates IO wait.

16:10 UTC - DB load remains high despite additional replica. Scaling back up thread pool configurations on JVM nodes as a stopgap measure.

16:30 UTC - Throughput stabilizing, but latency is still erratic. Will continue monitoring; scheduling deep-dive investigation into connection pool settings and DB query plans.

description: A routine RabbitMQ upgrade from 3.8.9 to 3.8.16 introduced a 15-minute consumer ack timeout that the changelog described as scoped to quorum queues but actually applied to all queue types. Consumers on the VM-destroyer queue gradually got their channels closed until the queue had zero consumers, so VMs in one region stopped being deleted; this eventually backed up VM creation and blocked Docker, machine, Windows, Mac, Arm, GPU, and remote-Docker jobs for ~12 hours.
raw_input:
14:10 UTC - Alert: RabbitMQ cluster upgrade complete. Nodes v1-v3 running 3.8.16.

14:22 UTC - PagerDuty: High error rate on 'VM-destroyer' service. Logs showing "Channel closed" and "PRECONDITION_FAILED" on connection attempts.

14:45 UTC - Observed sharp drop in message processing rate for 'VM-destroyer' queue. Consumer count for this specific queue has hit zero. Restarting service nodes has no effect; they connect and immediately drop.

15:15 UTC - Dashboard: VM-destroyer queue depth spiking rapidly. VM deletion tasks are not being picked up, causing capacity warnings in US-East region.

16:30 UTC - Escalation: VM deletion backlog is now impacting downstream provisioning. Users reporting "Resource limit reached" for new machine jobs.

18:00 UTC - Scope broadening: Backlog in 'VM-destroyer' is creating a ripple effect. Docker, GPU, and Windows runner pools are hitting max capacity. New jobs failing across multiple regions.

21:45 UTC - Investigation focus shifting to RabbitMQ middleware. Comparing current consumer behavior against pre-upgrade metrics. Channels are being killed exactly 15 minutes after initialization. 

01:20 UTC - Discovered all queue types are experiencing forced disconnects after 15 minutes of idle/processing time. Manually throttling incoming requests to prevent total system saturation while we revert nodes.

description: A staged Kubernetes upgrade of CircleCI's main production cluster left `kube-proxy` and `kubelet` at incompatible versions. The change between versions altered the format of `kube-proxy`'s `iptables` rulesets, so as pods churned and `Endpoints` objects changed, `kube-proxy`'s `Proxier.syncProxyRules()` (an `iptables-save` / `iptables-restore` read-modify-write) repeatedly hit "Sync failed" errors, leaving the per-node iptables in a corrupted state and silently breaking service-to-service routing across the cluster. Recovery required a full node-by-node cluster restart and triggered two follow-on incidents.
raw_input:
14:12 UTC - PagerDuty alert: High 5xx error rate on internal API gateway. Threshold exceeded.
14:15 - Investigating: Latency spiking globally across service-to-service communication. Database connections appear fine, but upstream services are reporting "connection refused" or timeouts.
14:22 - Noticed a surge in "Sync failed" errors in kube-proxy logs across multiple nodes.
14:30 - Incident declared. Traffic routing is erratic; some nodes appear to be successfully passing traffic while others are completely blackholed.
14:45 - kubectl get endpoints shows correct data, but curl tests between pods confirm packet loss.
15:05 - Verified iptables rules are failing to update; consistent "Resource temporarily unavailable" or malformed ruleset errors appearing in system logs on affected nodes.
15:20 - Attempting to cordon and drain one affected node to see if a fresh pod startup resolves routing.
15:35 - Draining did not fix the host. The node is still unable to route traffic to new pods. Escalating to Infrastructure team; suspecting underlying CNI or proxy daemon failure.

description: A parser bug caused Cloudflare edge servers to return memory that contained private information such as HTTP cookies, authentication tokens, HTTP POST bodies, and other sensitive data.

08:19 UTC - Investigating potential origin reachability issues; latency looking normal but error rate climbing on specific server clusters.

08:24 UTC - Internal dashboard shows memory usage on edge servers trending significantly higher than baseline; some processes crashing with OOM errors.

08:35 UTC - Customer report via Twitter/Support: A user claims to see unrelated content in their browser response body after a request.

08:42 UTC - Initial triage suggests this isn't a typical DDoS or origin flap; behavior is non-deterministic and affects random request bodies.

08:58 UTC - Disabling suspect canary release on a subset of nodes; monitoring for drop in 502s and memory pressure.

09:12 UTC - Customer support volume increasing; reports of sensitive data leakage confirmed in multiple payloads.

09:15 UTC - Escalating to Engineering lead: Initiating emergency stop on all edge traffic routing through the suspected parser modules.

description: A CPU exhaustion was caused by a single WAF rule that contained a poorly written regular expression that ended up creating excessive backtracking. This rule was deployed quickly to production and a series of events lead to a global 27 minutes downtime of the Cloudflare services.

14:10 UTC - PagerDuty alert: Global CPU usage across edge nodes spiking > 90%. High latency reported on edge processing.

14:12 - Dashboard showing 5xx error rates climbing rapidly across all regions. Traffic dropping significantly.

14:37 - Services stabilizing after rolling back the latest WAF configuration update. Error rates returning to baseline.

description: A Content update containing undetected errors was deployed due to a bug in the Content Validator in the deployment stage. This problematic content caused an out-of-bounds memory read, resulting in a Windows operating system crash (BSOD) on 8.5 million Windows machines. The update was reverted within 78 minutes, but the incident highlighted the need for improved validation and testing processes.

description: After an automatic upgrade, all network rules were removed and caused a 24h duration outage of all their Cilium protected Kubernetes clusters in all their regions and cloud providers.

description: "At approximately 14:01, a Redis instance acting as the primary for a highly-available cluster used by Discord's API services was migrated automatically by Google’s Cloud Platform. This migration caused the node to incorrectly drop offline, forcing the cluster to rebalance and trigger known issues with the way Discord API instances handle Redis failover. After resolving this partial outage, unnoticed issues on other services caused a cascading failure through Discord’s real time system. These issues caused enough critical impact that Discord’s engineering team was forced to fully restart the service, reconnecting millions of clients over a period of 20 minutes."
raw_input:
14:01 UTC - PagerDuty: API Latency spike detected. P99s exceeding 2s for core gateways.

description: An overflow occurred when converting a 16-bit number to a 64-bit numer in the Ariane 5 intertial guidance system, causing the rocket to crash. description: MongoDB fell over under load when it ran out of memory. The failure was catastrophic and not graceful due to a a query pattern that involved a read-load with low levels of locality (each user check-in caused a read of all check-ins for the user's history, and records were 300 bytes with no spatial locality, meaning that most of the data pulled in from each page was unnecessary). A lack of monitoring on the MongoDB instances caused the high load to go undetected until the load became catastrophic, causing 17 hours of downtime spanning two incidents in two days.

description: Filestore enforces a global limit on API requests to limit impact in overload scenarios. The outage was triggered when an internal Google service managing a large number of GCP projects malfunctioned and overloaded the Filestore API with requests, causing global throttling of the Filestore API. This continued until the internal service was manually paused. As a result of this throttling, read-only API access was unavailable for all customers. This affected customers in all locations, due to a global quota that applies to Filestore. Console, gcloud and API access (List, GetOperation, etc.) calls all failed for a duration of 3 hours, 12 minutes. Mutate operations (CreateInstance, UpdateInstance, CreateBackup, etc.) still succeeded, but customers were unable to check on operation progress.

description: A policy change containing blank fields triggered a null pointer exception in Service Control, Google's API management and control plane system. The code path that failed was not feature flag protected and lacked proper error handling. When the policy data replicated globally, it caused Service Control binaries to crash loop across all regions. While a red-button fix was deployed within 40 minutes, larger regions like us-central-1 experienced extended recovery times (up to 2h 40m) due to a thundering herd problem when Service Control tasks restarted, overloading the underlying Spanner infrastructure. The incident affected Google and Google Cloud APIs globally, with recovery times varying by product architecture.

description: A change to the core application that manages the underlying infrastructure for the Common Runtime included a dependency upgrade that caused a timing lock issue that greatly reduced the throughput of our task workers. This dependency change, coupled with a failure to appropriately scale up due to increased workload scheduling, caused the application's work queue to build up. Contributing to the issue, the team was not alerted immediately that new router instances were not being initialized correctly on startup largely because of incorrectly configured alerts. These router instances were serving live traffic already but were shown to be in the wrong boot state, and they were deleted via our normal processes due to failing readiness checks. The deletion caused a degradation of the associated runtime cluster while the autoscaling group was creating new instances. This reduced pool of router instances caused requests to fail as more requests were coming in faster than the limited number of routers could handle. This is when customers started noticing issues with the service.

description: After moving to Google Cloud they saw spikes of Postgres connection timeouts (~200 new connections/s) and memcached "i/o timeout" errors. Pool tuning (15m→30m max lifetime, static pool size, `MaxIdleConns` 2→20) helped each in turn but didn't eliminate the cache errors. The smoking gun was GKE Dataplane V2: bursts of parallel outbound calls (made worse by an accidental N+1 join) saturated the per-node `anetd` agent's CPU, dropping packets between the node and other services running on it.

14:55 - Observing packet loss between pods on the same node during traffic bursts. Suspecting network congestion or CNI issue.

15:10 - Identifying an inefficient N+1 query pattern in the service logic that is triggering massive parallel outbound request bursts. Mitigating via code revert/kill switch.

A traffic-management feature flag also failed to apply globally because of a recent "top 10 orgs by volume" usability tweak.

description: One night in July 2012, a skewed electricity supply-demand profile developed when the northern grid drew a tremendous amount of power from the western and eastern grids. Following a series of circuit breakers tripping by virtue of under-frequency protection, the entire NEW (northern-eastern-western) grid collapsed due to the absence of islanding mechanisms. While the grid was reactivated after over 8 hours, similar conditions in the following day caused the grid to fail again. However, the restoration effort concluded almost 24 hours after the occurrence of the latter incident.

description: A scripting bug caused the generation of the divider logic in the Pentium to very occasionally produce incorrect results. The bug wasn't caught in testing because of an incorrect assumption in a proof of correctness. (See [the Wikipedia article on 1994 FDIV bug](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pentium_FDIV_bug) for more information.)

09:35 PST - Customer reports similar precision drift in scientific modeling suite. Reproduced internally on P5 architecture machines. Values differ from software-emulated results.

10:05 PST - Alerting: Spike in support tickets tagged "calculation error" and "precision issue." Filtering for CPU-specific hardware requests.

10:48 PST - Escalated to Hardware Engineering. Suspecting potential microcode or FPU pipeline issue. Need to check if specific instruction patterns trigger the divergence.

11:20 PST - Verified: Issue is isolated to FDIV operations on current production silicon. Error is extremely subtle and non-deterministic across varied inputs. 

12:15 PST - Internal test suite is passing basic parity checks. Need to develop a specialized stress test for the divider circuitry to narrow down the range of inputs causing incorrect bits.

13:40 PST - Confirmed: Issue persists across multiple units of the same stepping. Engineering pull request to halt further silicon deployment until diagnostic results are conclusive.

description: A design flaw in the Apollo 11 rendezvous radar produced excess CPU load, causing the spacecraft computer to restart during lunar landing.

description: NASA's Mars Pathfinder spacecraft experienced system resets a few days after landing on Mars (1997). The OS software was remotely patched (all the way to Mars) to fix the problem by adding priority inheritance to the task scheduler.
